title: What software architecture actually is
intro: Four minutes to separate architecture from the technologies people confuse it with.

steps.title: Structure is judged against intent
steps.body:
A **theatre** and a **suburban home** are both good buildings — but only relative to what they're *for*. Acoustics and sightlines for one; daily living for the other.

Software is the same. There is no universally 'correct' architecture — only one that fits an **explicit intent**. A CRUD web app and a real-time voice platform are both right, for different purposes.

> [!key] Architecture is the **high-level description of a system's components and how they communicate** to meet its requirements and constraints.

steps.title: Technologies are NOT architecture
steps.body:
The single most common mistake: 'we're using React + Postgres + Kafka' is *not* an architecture — those are **implementation details**.

Decide **topology and protocols first** (what the components are, how they talk). Defer language/framework/library choices to the **end**. A component is a **black box** defined by its interface, not its internal code.

steps.title: Design at the Service level
steps.body:
Complexity climbs a ladder — and global systems are reasoned about near the top:

Method → Class → Module → Library → Application → **System (services)**.

Millions of users, high throughput and distributed data are orchestrated at the **Service** level, not the class level. Tap the top rung:

viz.title: Levels of abstraction
viz.prompt: Tap the level where you design for scale.

points.label: Method
points.note: A single function — far too fine-grained for a global system.

points.label: Service
points.note: Processes / deployable groups — THIS is the level of large-scale design.

steps.title: Where it sits — and why it's risky
steps.body:
Architecture lives in the **Design** phase, before Implementation → Testing → Deployment.

> [!warn] A design can't be *proven* correct up front. Get it wrong and you face a brutal, expensive rewrite. Architects manage this risk with known patterns, organised processes, and decision records — not proofs.

recap:
**Architecture = components + how they communicate, chosen to fit an explicit intent.**

- Structure is judged against **purpose**, not in the abstract.
- **Technologies are implementation details** — decide topology/protocols first, tech last.
- Design large systems at the **Service** level.
- It's a **Design-phase** activity, and it's high-stakes: wrong choices are costly to undo.
